texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nondispersive absorption of CO2 into an aqueous solution of a volatile amine and its subsequent regeneration by stripping in porous hydrophobic hollow fiber devices have encountered a number of issues: membrane pore wetting reducing CO2 transfer rates; loss of volatile absorbents. These issues have been addressed here using novel nonvolatile absorbents and novel hollow fiber membranes. Continuous CO2 absorption -stripping was carried out for 55 days using a novel absorbent, polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer of generation 0, in an aqueous solution using conventional Celgard polypropylene (PP) hollow fibers in an absorber followed by a stripper to successfully resolve the issue of pore wetting. This nonvolatile amine PAMAM provides a novel dimension to CO2 scrubbing using conventional porous PP membrane contactors. Elimination of pore wetting and reduction of loss of the volatile monoethanolamine (MEA) from an aqueous solution was studied for an extended period of more than 55 days using an absorber and a stripper made out of porous poly(4-methyl-1-pentene) (PMP) hollow fibers having an ultrathin dense skin. The dense PMP fiber skin could not eliminate completely the loss of MEA. The PMP membrane resistance was an important factor in CO2 absorption along with the MEA concentration.

texts.orig: Such approaches have led to additional issues. The studies by Li and Teo 10 using an ultrathin nonporous skin based polyethersulfone hollow fiber membrane indicate that the CO2 absorption rate is drastically reduced compared to that in hydrophobic porous polypropylene (PP) fibers. Thus nonporous membranes are needed with higher CO2 transport rates. Nonvolatile amino acids in aqueous solutions are known to crystallize at high CO2 loadings. 12 Are there other nonvolatile absorbents without such deficiencies? Porous PTFEbased membranes cost as much as 10 times PP costs; further, the surface areas per unit equipment volume for PTFE-based membrane modules are significantly smaller than those of PP-based hollow fiber modules. The search for more efficient/cheaper membranes/fibers continues.

texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas mixture separation and purification is conventionally implemented by a number of separation processes: gas absorption into a solvent, pressure swing adsorption, cryogenic separation, membrane permeation, etc. One of the most widely used techniques is gas absorption into an absorbent solution; usually large scrubbing towers are used where one of the phases is dispersed in the other either as a drop, a bubble, or a thin film. A microporous/porous membrane based nondispersive gas absorption technique has been introduced recently (for a review, see Sirkar, 1 Reed et al., 2 and Gabelman and Hwang 3 ). Such a technique employs a porous hydrophobic membrane absorber/contactor where the gas and the absorbent solution flow on two sides of a porous gas-filled membrane without being dispersed as bubbles, thin films, drops, etc. As long as the correct phase pressure difference is maintained, 4 the membrane pores do not get wetted and neither the gas nor the liquid is dispersed into the other phase. Employment of hollow fine fibers to provide a high contacting surface area per unit equipment volume and shell-side crossflow to yield a high liquid-side mass transfer coefficient has led to successful commercialization of this technique for gas absorption/stripping. 5

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have studied the absorption -stripping behavior of a 21.6 vol % aqueous solution of this nonvolatile amine using conventional porous PP hollow fiber membranes. The objectives were to find out whether the fibers get wetted over an extended period of operation (55 days) and what are the CO2 transfer rates and mass transfer coefficients. The results of this successful experiment were analyzed by measuring the surface tension behavior of this aqueous solution over a very wide range of dendrimer concentrations. It is useful to note that our studies using pure generation 0 dendrimer liquid with or without glycerol as an immobilized liquid membrane in porous substrates demonstrated an extraordinarily high CO2 -N2 selectivity (14 000 -18 000 at low CO2 concentrations and around 700 at higher CO2 concentrations). 13,14

texts.orig: Approach. To study the absorber -stripper behavior for continuous CO2 absorption, experiments were carried out with different hollow fiber modules fabricated using either porous hydrophobic Celgard X-10 polypropylene or poly(4-methyl-1-pentene) hollow fibers. Deionized water, an aqueous solution of dendrimer, and an aqueous solution of monoethanolamine were used separately as the absorbent. A humidified gas mixture of 5% CO2 in N2 (balance) was used as the feed, and humidified helium (zero) was used as the stripping gas. In all experiments, the feed gas was passed through the shell side and the absorbent was passed countercurrently through the tube side (Figure 2). Solutions

texts.orig: Hollow Fiber Modules. One set of two modules for CO2 absorption and CO2 stripping was fabricated from hollow fiber membranes supplied by Celgard (Charlotte, NC); the other set of two modules having the same absorber/stripper area ratio was prepared using the poly(4-methyl-1-pentene) hollow fiber membranes supplied by DIC Inc. (Tokyo, Japan). Another module was prepared using Celgard fibers for initial unsteady state CO2 absorption studies. Each module was tested for leak-free operation with water prior to use. Specifications for all of the modules are provided in Table 1.

texts.orig: The experiments were started by first passing the absorbent liquid through the tube side of the modules followed by passing the feed and sweep gases countercurrently on the shell side. The feed and sweep gas flow rates were held constant at 8.7 and 61.3 cm 3 /min, respectively. The absorbent liquid was also allowed to flow at a constant rate of 6.8 cm 3 /min for runs in all systems unless otherwise mentioned. Runs for each system were continued for a long time (50 -55 days) by maintaining constant flow rates throughout the period; the extent of CO2 absorption was frequently analyzed. Later, the absorbent liquid flow rate was changed to other values and held constant until steady state was reached.

texts.orig: Additional studies were continued for more than 70 days in the PMP fiber based modules fabricated using the same absorber/stripper area ratio as that of the Celgard fiber based modules by using an aqueous solution of monoethanolamine as absorbent. The experimental run was started with 11.6 vol % MEA solution (equivalent to that of the dendrimer solution used), and the permeation of MEA from the tube side to the shell side was monitored periodically by analyzing the absorbent samples collected from the reservoir. After 65 days, pure MEA was added to the reservoir to bring the absorbent concentration to the level of the original value and the treated retentate gas was analyzed for different absorbent flow rates.

texts.orig: Analytical Measurements. A HP 5890 series II (Hewlett-Packard, Wilmington, DE) gas chromatograph (GC) was used to determine the composition of the gas streams using a Porapak Q 80/100 (6 ft length, 0.085 in. i.d., and 1 /8 in. o.d.) column. When MEA was used as the absorbent, the loss or permeation of the absorbent through the pores of the fibers from the tube side to the shell side of the module was monitored by analyzing the liquid samples collected from the reservoir using another GC (HP 6890 series). The column in this GC was HP-1, 30 m long, 0.53 mm diameter, and 3 mthickness. A headspace sampler (HP 7694 Headspace Sampler) was used to inject the samples. The GC was calibrated for the concentration range of 2.5 -12.5 vol % MEA.

texts.orig: Surface Tension Measurements. An aqueous solution of 30 cm 3 containing 40 vol % dendrimer was prepared from 13.94 g of the pure dendrimer. Solutions of 30, 20, 15, 10, and 5% dendrimer were also prepared by diluting respectively the 40, 30, 20, 15, and 10% solutions. Aqueous solutions containing 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, and 40 vol % MEA and EDA were also prepared. The surface tensions of the prepared solutions were measured by an interfacial tensiometer (model K8, Kruss, Hamburg, Germany).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We will first present and discuss the results of CO2 absorption -stripping studies with the dendrimer solution carried out for an extended period using Celgard PP hollow-fiber modules. We will next present and analyze the CO2 absorption -stripping studies carried out for an extended period using PMP fibers and MEAbased solution. Data for the limited studies on absorbent flow variation will be considered next and compared with what is available in the literature. The results of these studies will be then partly evaluated in the context of the wetting characteristics of the absorbent solution.

texts.orig: The absorber -stripper behavior for CO2 absorption using a 21.6 vol % aqueous solution of dendrimer was studied next for an extended period of 55 days in Celgard hollow fiber modules having a constant flow rate of the aqueous solution of dendrimer. The extent of absorption of CO2 was found to be essentially constant throughout the experimental period. The CO2 concentration in the gas stream at the absorber outlet and the CO2 mass transfer coefficient are plotted against time in Figure 5. The scatter in the data is most likely due to the temperature variation in the laboratory. These results indicate that the properties of the dendrimer solution remained unchanged throughout the experiment. Further, the membrane pores remain unchanged: the aqueous solution of the dendrimer did not wet the pores of the Celgard fibers, nor was there any loss of dendrimer because of its negligibly low vapor pressure, if any.

texts.orig: CO2 concentration in the gas stream at the absorber outlet (before reaching the steady state) was very low; these results are shown in Figure 6. As time progressed, the CO2 outlet concentration increased, reaching a steady state. Correspondingly, the CO2 absorption rate decreased at steady state. Obviously, the stripping arrangement was quite insufficient to maintain a very high level of CO2 removal during the steady state operation. This leads to the conclusion that the aqueous solution of dendrimer can be used successfully for the removal of low and high concentrations of CO2 from gas mixtures by using better stripping conditions. These include higher stripping membrane area, higher stripping temperature, and higher mass transfer coefficients via higher velocity as well as cross-flow in the stripper.

texts.orig: The pressures in the tube side of the modules are shown in Table 2. At the end of the experimental period, a very small increase in pressure drop in the tube side was observed at the inlets of both absorber and stripper. It was found that this increase in the pressures was due to the deposition of dirt, etc., at the tube sheet of the hollow fibers, suggesting the need for prefiltration. Moreover, the pressure drop was found to be constant throughout the experimental period. Experiments were also conducted for a short period of time with water as absorbent and with the same absorbent velocity of 6.24 cm/s. A significant difference was observed in the CO2 concentration coming out of the absorber between the dendrimer solution and water as absorbents; the values are listed in Table 3. In the case of water as absorbent, the mass transfer coefficient was not calculated because of a lack of CO2 concentration data in the liquid phase. The velocity of the liquid solution was 6.24 cm/s. This is a sufficiently low value for tube side flow; this accounts for the lower values of the CO2 transfer coefficients. However, much higher liquid-side mass transfer coefficients can be achieved if the absorbent is in cross-flow in the shell side over the fibers; this value can go up by as much as an order of magnitude at relatively low Reynolds numbers.

texts.orig: The van der Waals volume of methanol calculated by group contribution method is about 22 cm 3 /mol, 22 with the corresponding diameter of a hypothetical sphere of about 4.1 Å which is close to the kinetic diameter of 3.8 Å found from diffusion of methanol in zeolites. 23 The group contribution method gives the van der Waals volume of MEA of about 39 cm 3 /mol, 22 which yields an average diameter of 5 Å if one approximates this molecule as a sphere. The kinetic cross section of the molecule should be less than this value due to the geometry of the molecule and additional degrees of freedom because of rotation around the C -N bond. We assume the kinetic cross section to be 4.5 Å, i.e., still allowing the MEA molecule to penetrate through the microvoid elements of PMP dense structure.

The vapor pressure of pure MEA at 20 °C was considered to be equal to 0.67 mmHg. 24 For a membrane area of 236.5 cm 2 and applying Raoult's law, MEA permeance was found to be equal to 3.0 10 -4 cm 3 (STP)/(cm 2 cmHg s). This is higher than that of methanol. However, one should also take into consideration the fact that such a long period of PMP conditioning with MEA can result in a slight swelling of the polymer with a substantial increase in transport, as happens in the case of limonene swelling. 25 PMP has a solubility parameter of about 16 (MPa) 1/2 ; 26 for methanol and MEA those values are equal to 29.7 and 31.5 (MPa) 1/2 , respectively. 27 Such a large difference in solubility constants for the polymer and the solvents shows that substantial swelling is not probable there. However, a minimal amount of swelling still can take place, namely after a long conditioning of the polymer.

texts.orig: One may apply the traditional solution -diffusion model to describe the transport of methanol and MEA through the dense PMP skin. The volume of the largest cavity in PMP is about 65 Å 3 ; van der Waals volumes of methanol and MEA are about 37 and 65 Å 3 , respectively. This means that the MEA molecule can fit very well into such a microvoid, which results in both a large solubility coefficient of MEA in PMP and correspondingly high excess enthalpy of mixing in comparison to sorbate molecules having sizes larger and smaller than that of MEA. Such a behavior was observed earlier for amorphous perfluoropolymers AF, having even a larger void volume element corresponding to undecane as sorbate having an optimal volume vis-a `-vis the void volume in AF2400. 28 Therefore, there is a possibility that the much larger solubility coefficient of MEA than that of methanol and comparable diffusion coefficients of both solvents can lead to a larger permeance of MEA compared to methanol. However, this postulate requires systematic studies.

texts.orig: The absorption of CO2 remained constant for a long time even though the concentration of the absorbent MEA in the liquid was getting reduced. This observed phenomenon leads to the conclusion that the membrane resistance was possibly reduced due to the presence of the MEA in the pores. MEA present in the pores while permeating from the tube side to the shell side is acting as a carrier for CO2, and thus reducing the membrane resistance. Other experiments carried out in our laboratory strongly suggest this behavior. From Table 3, the 21.6 vol % dendrimer solution yields a much larger CO2 mass transfer coefficient with Celgard fibers compared to the 11.6 vol % MEA solution with PMP fibers. It appears that even though we provided an almost equivalent amount of amines in the MEA and dendrimer solutions, the PMP fiber's wall resistance must have limited the CO2 transport rate. Alternately, one could argue that, at the given flow rate, the membrane resistance was controlling and initially there was an excess of MEA present in the solution.

texts.orig: For this extended run, the CO2 concentration in the gas stream at the absorber outlet and the mass transfer coefficients have been plotted against time and are shown in Figure 7. The pressures in the tube side of the modules are shown in Table 2. After 55 days, a gradual increase in the CO2 concentration in the gas stream at the absorber outlet was observed. These observed results may be explained as follows. The CO2 absorption started decreasing when the amine concentration in the reservoir reached a certain level where a decrease in the membrane resistance could not counter the effect of a decrease in the amine concentration. As the MEA concentration in the absorbent liquid decreased from 11.6% to 5.5%, facilitation of CO2 transport by the presence of MEA in the membrane pores could also have been decreased because the amount of MEA in the pores will correspond to the MEA concentration in the absorbent liquid. Alternately, at the prevailing membrane resistance, the decrease of amine concentration became rate-controlling. The CO2 in the gas stream at the absorber outlet had gone up to 3.9 vol %.

texts.orig: Surface Tensions of Aqueous Solutions of Various Absorbents. Since the surface tension of the absorbent liquid influences the pore wetting, the surface tensions of aqueous solutions of different absorbents were measured. The measured surface tensions for different volume fractions of aqueous solutions of dendrimer, MEA, and EDA are plotted against the corresponding volume fractions in Figure 9. Literature values for the surface tension of aqueous solutions of MEA are in good agreement with the measured values. 29 A small difference in the behavior at low volume fractions was observed in the case of dendrimer when compared with those of MEA and EDA. For aqueous solutions of dendrimer, the surface tension did not decrease so rapidly at lower volume fractions as it did for other aqueous solutions. It was also observed that 21.6 vol % dendrimer solution has a much higher surface tension than the 11.6 vol % aqueous solution of MEA.

texts.orig: It appears from the measurements that, by themselves, none of these solutions will approach the critical surface tension for polypropylene ( C ) 29 dyn cm -1 30 ) and PMP ( C ) 25 dyn cm -1 30 ). These solutions ought not to wet the pores of Celgard fibers. However, volatilization of MEA or DEA and their subsequent sorption on the polypropylene (PP) surface with the nonpolar part of the molecule adsorbed on the PP and the polar part sticking out is likely to alter the SL values substantially. Dendrimer solutions are unlikely to introduce such problems with porous fibers since they are effectively nonvolatile. In the present application, the pressures on two sides of the porous membranes were not too far apart. If however, the pressures are considerably apart, then chances of pore penetration by the liquid solution will increase as the breakthrough pressure difference is approached. 1
texts.text: It appears from the measurements that, by themselves, none of these solutions will approach the critical surface tension for polypropylene ( C ) 29 dyn cm -1 30 ) and PMP ( C ) 25 dyn cm -1 30 ). These solutions ought not to wet the pores of Celgard fibers. However, volatilization of MEA or DEA and their subsequent sorption on the polypropylene (PP) surface with the nonpolar part of the molecule adsorbed on the PP and the polar part sticking out is likely to alter the SL values substantially. Dendrimer solutions are unlikely to introduce such problems with porous fibers since they are effectively nonvolatile. In the present application, the pressures on two sides of the porous membranes were not too far apart. If however, the pressures are considerably apart, then chances of pore penetration by the liquid solution will increase as the breakthrough pressure difference is approached. 1

texts.text: Concluding Remarks

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Continuous absorption of CO2 from a gas mixture through polymeric hydrophobic hollow fiber membranes was studied over an extended period of time in an absorber -stripper combination. For the conventional porous hydrophobic Celgard hollow fibers of polypropylene (PP), PAMAM dendrimer of generation 0 was used as the reactive absorbent in water. A 55-day-long run demonstrated a stable performance with no wetting of the pores of porous PP fibers by the dendrimer. Studies with the asymmetric poly(4-methyl-1-pentene) (PMP) hollow fiber membranes having a dense skin and an aqueous solution of monoethanolamine (MEA) in water indicated significant permeation of MEA through the dense skin of the PMP fiber, resulting in a decreased CO2 absorption performance after 55 days.
texts.text: Continuous absorption of CO2 from a gas mixture through polymeric hydrophobic hollow fiber membranes was studied over an extended period of time in an absorber -stripper combination. For the conventional porous hydrophobic Celgard hollow fibers of polypropylene (PP), PAMAM dendrimer of generation 0 was used as the reactive absorbent in water. A 55-day-long run demonstrated a stable performance with no wetting of the pores of porous PP fibers by the dendrimer. Studies with the asymmetric poly(4-methyl-1-pentene) (PMP) hollow fiber membranes having a dense skin and an aqueous solution of monoethanolamine (MEA) in water indicated significant permeation of MEA through the dense skin of the PMP fiber, resulting in a decreased CO2 absorption performance after 55 days.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall CO2 mass transport coefficients in these CO2-scrubbing studies using low values of fluid velocities in parallel flow were 2.15 10 -3 cm/s for the first system and 9.57 10 -4 cm/s in the second PMP-based system. The mass transfer coefficient in the PMP modules appears to be controlled primarily by the membrane resistance. The mass transfer coefficient values obtained have also been compared with the results reported in the literature for pure water as an absorbent in modules employing Celgard as well as other fibers.

texts.orig: The dense skin on the PMP fiber has voids having dimensions in the range of 4.3 -5 Å. The openings of such dimensions can just about accommodate an MEA molecule and therefore allow MEA permeation and a slow loss from the aqueous absorbent solution. However, this disadvantage of a slow loss over an extended period was balanced by the MEA penetration and a potential decrease of the membrane resistance to CO2 transport.
